resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Present tense conjugation of First Conjugation (-ати) verbs, pro-drop, imperfective aspect.
Not covered:
  - Second conjugation (-ити) verbs → a1-16
  - Questions and negation details → a1-18
-->

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Verbs are the driving force of any language. Mastering the most common conjugation pattern unlocks your ability to describe your daily life, actions, and habits.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Віта́ємо на ново́му ета́пі! (Welcome to a new stage!) 
Ми вже зна́ємо слова́ про ре́чі. (We already know words about things.)
Тепе́р час для ді́ї! (Now it is time for action!)
Сього́дні ми вивча́ємо дієсло́ва. (Today we are studying verbs.)
Дієсло́ва опи́сують на́ші ді́ї. (Verbs describe our actions.)

Until now, we've focused on naming things (nouns). Now it's time for action. Verbs are the engine of Ukrainian sentences—they describe what you do, feel, and experience every day. 

Ми вивча́ємо найважливі́ші слова́: чита́ти, писа́ти і працюва́ти. (We are studying the most important words: to read, to write, and to work.)
Це на́ше щоде́нне життя́. (This is our daily life.)
Ці слова́ потрі́бні для робо́ти і хо́бі. (These words are needed for work and hobbies.)

> [!culture]
> В Украї́ні є стара́ тради́ція. (In Ukraine there is an old tradition.)
> Пе́рша відо́ма кни́га — це «Апо́стол». (The first known book is the "Apostol".)
> Іва́н Фе́доров — відо́мий друка́р. (Ivan Fedorov is a famous printer.)
>
> The first known printed book in Ukraine, the *Apostol*, was published in Lviv in 1574. Because of this deep-rooted tradition, reading is a great starting point for our verbs!

Let's use **чита́ти** (to read) to see how Ukrainian verbs work. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to talk about your daily actions.

## Презентація (Presentation)

Украї́нські дієсло́ва ма́ють нові́ закінчення. (Ukrainian verbs have new endings.)
Це зале́жить від осо́би. (This depends on the person.)
В англі́йській мо́ві це іна́кше. (In English, this is different.)
Тут ко́жна осо́ба ма́є своє́ закінчення. (Here every person has its own ending.)

Це пра́вило назива́ється «дієвідмі́на». (This rule is called "conjugation".)
Більшість украї́нських дієслі́в закінчується на -ати. (Most Ukrainian verbs end in -ати.)
Це пе́рша дієвідмі́на. (This is the first conjugation.)
Напри́клад, сло́во «чита́ти». (For example, the word "читати".)

Verbs change their endings based on who is doing the action—this is called **conjugation**. The dictionary form of a verb is called the **infinitive**. Our first group of verbs are the "-ати" verbs, because their infinitive ends in those three letters, like **чита́ти** (to read).

Де осно́ва сло́ва? (Where is the word stem?)
Ми відкида́ємо закінчення -ти. (We drop the -ти ending.)
Осно́ва сло́ва «чита́ти» — «чита-». (The stem of the word "читати" is "чита-".)
До осно́ви ми додає́мо нові́ закінчення. (We add new endings to the stem.)

To conjugate a verb, simply drop the **-ти** from the infinitive to find the stem (чита-), and then add the ending that matches the person performing the action.

Ось нові́ закінчення. (Here are the new endings.)
- Я (I) takes the ending **-ю**.
- Ти (You, informal) takes the ending **-єш**.
- Він, вона́, воно́ (He, she, it) takes the ending **-є**.
- Ми (We) takes the ending **-ємо**.
- Ви (You, formal/plural) takes the ending **-єте**.
- Вони́ (They) take the ending **-ють**.

Let us apply this rule to our main verb.

| Person | Pronoun | Stem + Ending | Result | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------|--------|---------|
| I | я | чита- + -ю | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| You (informal) | ти | чита- + -єш | **ти чита́єш** | You read / You are reading |
| He/She/It | він/вона́/воно́ | чита- + -є | **він чита́є** | He reads / He is reading |
| We | ми | чита- + -ємо | **ми чита́ємо** | We read / We are reading |
| You (formal/plural) | ви | чита- + -єте | **ви чита́єте** | You read / You are reading |
| They | вони́ | чита- + -ють | **вони́ чита́ють** | They read / They are reading |

Це пра́вило ді́є для бага́тьох дієслі́в. (This rule works for many verbs.)
Якщо́ сло́во закінчується на -ати, ви вже зна́єте, як йо́го змінювати! (If a word ends in -ати, you already know how to change it!)

> [!note]
> Notice that Ukrainian verbs in the present tense do two jobs at once. They describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. The phrase **я чита́ю** means both "I read every day" and "I am reading right now". This is called the imperfective aspect. You do not need a completely separate tense for ongoing actions like you do in English.

В украї́нській мо́ві є секре́т. (In the Ukrainian language there is a secret.)
Займе́нники (я, ти, ми) мо́жна пропуска́ти. (Pronouns (I, you, we) can be omitted.)
Закінчення — це підка́зка. (The ending is a hint.)
Замі́сть «я чита́ю» ми ча́сто ка́жемо просто «чита́ю». (Instead of "я читаю" we often just say "читаю".)
Це звучи́ть ду́же приро́дно. (This sounds very natural.)

Let us look at another very common verb: **зна́ти** (to know). 
Ми відкида́ємо -ти. (We drop -ти.)
Осно́ва сло́ва — «зна-». (The word stem is "зна-".)

Я зна́ю все. (I know everything.)
Вона́ зна́є текст. (She knows the text.)
Ми не зна́ємо. (We do not know.)

## Практика (Practice)

Час для пра́ктики! (Time for practice!)
Ми вчи́мося на поми́лках. (We learn from mistakes.)
Ось типо́ві поми́лки. (Here are common mistakes.)

The first common mistake is "Infinitive Abuse". Because English verbs do not change much, learners often try to use the basic dictionary form in sentences. They try to translate "I read" word-for-word and say "Я читати." This is incorrect. It sounds like "I to read." You must always conjugate the verb to match the person.

Перша пробле́ма — це інфініти́в. (The first problem is the infinitive.)
В англі́йській мо́ві слова́ не змі́нюються. (In English, words do not change.)
Лю́ди ча́сто ка́жуть «я чита́ти». (People often say "I to read".)
Це поми́лка. (This is a mistake.)
Ми за́вжди змі́нюємо дієсло́во. (We always change the verb.)

Incorrect: Я розумі́ти все. (I to understand everything.)
Correct: Я розумі́ю все. (I understand everything.)

Incorrect: Ми чека́ти на авто́бус. (We to wait for the bus.)
Correct: Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. (We are waiting for the bus.)

The second common mistake is "Pronoun Overuse". When you speak English, you constantly say "I do this, I do that." If you repeat the word **я** before every single verb in Ukrainian, you will sound a bit robotic and unnatural. Remember the pro-drop rule we discussed earlier. You can safely drop the pronoun if the context is clear.

Ми чита́ємо ці ре́чення. (We are reading these sentences.)
Тут ми не пи́шемо сло́во «я». (Here we do not write the word "I".)
Так ми говори́мо приро́дно. (This is how we speak naturally.)
Ми чита́ємо їх вго́лос. (We read them aloud.)

Працю́ю вдо́ма. (I work at home.)
Працю́ю бага́то. (I work a lot.)
Працю́ю над проє́ктом. (I am working on a project.)
Слу́хаю джаз. (I am listening to jazz.)
Слу́хаю ува́жно. (I listen carefully.)
Ду́маю про це. (I am thinking about it.)
Ду́маю бага́то. (I think a lot.)
Гра́ю в і́гри. (I play games.)
Гра́ю на гіта́рі. (I play the guitar.)

> [!tip]
> Пропуска́ти за́йменники — це норма́льно. (Dropping pronouns is normal.)
> Без сло́ва «я» ва́ша мо́ва звучи́ть більш приро́дно. (Without the word "я", your speech sounds more natural.)

We can also build sentences with objects. For now, we will use objects in their correct forms without worrying about all the complex case rules—just learn these common combinations as set phrases to build sentences easily and confidently.

Чита́ю текст. (I am reading a text.)
Чита́ю вго́лос. (I am reading aloud.)
Чита́ю нови́ни. (I am reading the news.)
Пишу́ листа́. (I am writing a letter.)
Пишу́ повідо́млення. (I am writing a message.)
Га́рно пишу́. (I write beautifully.)
Слу́хаю ра́діо. (I am listening to the radio.)
Пита́ю про це. (I am asking about it.)
Чека́ю на ві́дповідь. (I am waiting for an answer.)
Вивча́ю мо́ву. (I am studying the language.)
Вивча́ю слова́. (I am studying words.)

Тепе́р ви мо́жете розказа́ти про свій день. (Now you can tell about your day.)
Напри́клад, як ви працю́єте чи відпочива́єте. (For example, how you work or rest.)

Відпочива́ю вдо́ма. (I rest at home.)
Відпочива́ю влі́тку. (I rest in the summer.)
Не розумі́ю. (I do not understand.)

By combining correctly conjugated verbs with simple objects or adverbs, you create full, meaningful sentences. You are now communicating real actions.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

Сього́дні ми ви́вчили бага́то но́вого. (Today we learned a lot of new things.)
Дієсло́ва пе́ршої дієвідмі́ни — це ключ до спілкува́ння. (First conjugation verbs are the key to communication.)

The First Conjugation pattern is a master key. By learning how to conjugate **чита́ти**, you instantly know how to conjugate hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs like **писа́ти**, **працюва́ти**, and **слу́хати**.

> [!did-you-know]
> В Украї́ні є відо́ме прислі́в'я: (In Ukraine there is a famous proverb:)
> Пти́цю пізнаю́ть по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну — по мо́ві. (A bird is known by its feathers, and a person by their speech.)
>

Тепе́р ви мо́жете: (Now you can:)
- Говори́ти про регуля́рні та пото́чні ді́ї. (Talk about regular and current actions.)
- Будува́ти ре́чення без за́йменників. (Build sentences without pronouns.)

You learned that verbs in the present tense describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. There is no need to worry about complex tense structures just yet. You also learned how to drop unnecessary pronouns to make your sentences flow naturally like a native speaker. Now, you can confidently describe your daily activities. You can say that you work, you read, you rest, and you understand. 

Test your knowledge with these quick questions. If you can answer them, you are perfectly ready for the next step.

1. Яке́ закінчення для «ми»? (What is the ending for "we"?) — Закі́нчення **-ємо**. (The ending is -ємо.)

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Verbs are the driving force of any language. Mastering the most common conjugation pattern unlocks your ability to describe your daily life, actions, and habits.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Віта́ємо на ново́му ета́пі! (Welcome to a new stage!) 
Ми вже зна́ємо слова́ про ре́чі. (We already know words about things.)
Тепе́р час для ді́ї! (Now it is time for action!)
Сього́дні ми вивча́ємо дієсло́ва. (Today we are studying verbs.)
Дієсло́ва опи́сують на́ші ді́ї. (Verbs describe our actions.)

Until now, we've focused on naming things (nouns). Now it's time for action. Verbs are the engine of Ukrainian sentences—they describe what you do, feel, and experience every day. 

Ми вивча́ємо найважливі́ші слова́: чита́ти, писа́ти і працюва́ти. (We are studying the most important words: to read, to write, and to work.)
Це на́ше щоде́нне життя́. (This is our daily life.)
Ці слова́ потрі́бні для робо́ти і хо́бі. (These words are needed for work and hobbies.)

> [!culture]
> В Украї́ні є стара́ тради́ція. (In Ukraine there is an old tradition.)
> Пе́рша відо́ма кни́га — це «Апо́стол». (The first known book is the "Apostol".)
> Іва́н Фе́доров — відо́мий друка́р. (Ivan Fedorov is a famous printer.)
>
> The first known printed book in Ukraine, the *Apostol*, was published in Lviv in 1574. Because of this deep-rooted tradition, reading is a great starting point for our verbs!

Let's use **чита́ти** (to read) to see how Ukrainian verbs work. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to talk about your daily actions.

## Презентація (Presentation)

Украї́нські дієсло́ва ма́ють нові́ закінчення. (Ukrainian verbs have new endings.)
Це зале́жить від осо́би. (This depends on the person.)
В англі́йській мо́ві це іна́кше. (In English, this is different.)
Тут ко́жна осо́ба ма́є своє́ закінчення. (Here every person has its own ending.)

Це пра́вило назива́ється «дієвідмі́на». (This rule is called "conjugation".)
Більшість украї́нських дієслі́в закінчується на -ати. (Most Ukrainian verbs end in -ати.)
Це пе́рша дієвідмі́на. (This is the first conjugation.)
Напри́клад, сло́во «чита́ти». (For example, the word "читати".)

Verbs change their endings based on who is doing the action—this is called **conjugation**. The dictionary form of a verb is called the **infinitive**. Our first group of verbs are the "-ати" verbs, because their infinitive ends in those three letters, like **чита́ти** (to read).

Де осно́ва сло́ва? (Where is the word stem?)
Ми відкида́ємо закінчення -ти. (We drop the -ти ending.)
Осно́ва сло́ва «чита́ти» — «чита-». (The stem of the word "читати" is "чита-".)
До осно́ви ми додає́мо нові́ закінчення. (We add new endings to the stem.)

To conjugate a verb, simply drop the **-ти** from the infinitive to find the stem (чита-), and then add the ending that matches the person performing the action.

Ось нові́ закінчення. (Here are the new endings.)
- Я (I) takes the ending **-ю**.
- Ти (You, informal) takes the ending **-єш**.
- Він, вона́, воно́ (He, she, it) takes the ending **-є**.
- Ми (We) takes the ending **-ємо**.
- Ви (You, formal/plural) takes the ending **-єте**.
- Вони́ (They) take the ending **-ють**.

Let us apply this rule to our main verb.

| Person | Pronoun | Stem + Ending | Result | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------|--------|---------|
| I | я | чита- + -ю | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| You (informal) | ти | чита- + -єш | **ти чита́єш** | You read / You are reading |
| He/She/It | він/вона́/воно́ | чита- + -є | **він чита́є** | He reads / He is reading |
| We | ми | чита- + -ємо | **ми чита́ємо** | We read / We are reading |
| You (formal/plural) | ви | чита- + -єте | **ви чита́єте** | You read / You are reading |
| They | вони́ | чита- + -ють | **вони́ чита́ють** | They read / They are reading |

Це пра́вило ді́є для бага́тьох дієслі́в. (This rule works for many verbs.)
Сло́во закінчується на -ати? (Does the word end in -ати?) Ви зна́єте пра́вило! (You know the rule!)

> [!note]
> Notice that Ukrainian verbs in the present tense do two jobs at once. They describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. The phrase **я чита́ю** means both "I read every day" and "I am reading right now". This is called the imperfective aspect. You do not need a completely separate tense for ongoing actions like you do in English.

В украї́нській мо́ві є секре́т. (In the Ukrainian language there is a secret.)
Займе́нники (я, ти, ми) мо́жна пропуска́ти. (Pronouns (I, you, we) can be omitted.)
Закінчення — це підка́зка. (The ending is a hint.)
Замі́сть «я чита́ю» ми ча́сто ка́жемо просто «чита́ю». (Instead of "я читаю" we often just say "читаю".)
Це звучи́ть ду́же приро́дно. (This sounds very natural.)

Let us look at another very common verb: **зна́ти** (to know). 
Ми відкида́ємо -ти. (We drop -ти.)
Осно́ва сло́ва — «зна-». (The word stem is "зна-".)

Я зна́ю все. (I know everything.)
Вона́ зна́є текст. (She knows the text.)
Ми не зна́ємо. (We do not know.)

## Практика (Practice)

Час для пра́ктики! (Time for practice!)
Ми вчи́мося на поми́лках. (We learn from mistakes.)
Ось типо́ві поми́лки. (Here are common mistakes.)

The first common mistake is "Infinitive Abuse". Because English verbs do not change much, learners often try to use the basic dictionary form in sentences. They try to translate "I read" word-for-word and say "Я читати." This is incorrect. It sounds like "I to read." You must always conjugate the verb to match the person.

Перша пробле́ма — це інфініти́в. (The first problem is the infinitive.)
В англі́йській мо́ві слова́ не змі́нюються. (In English, words do not change.)
Лю́ди ча́сто ка́жуть «я чита́ти». (People often say "I to read".)
Це поми́лка. (This is a mistake.)
Ми за́вжди змі́нюємо дієсло́во. (We always change the verb.)

Incorrect: Я розумі́ти все. (I to understand everything.)
Correct: Я розумі́ю все. (I understand everything.)

Incorrect: Ми чека́ти на авто́бус. (We to wait for the bus.)
Correct: Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. (We are waiting for the bus.)

The second common mistake is "Pronoun Overuse". When you speak English, you constantly say "I do this, I do that." If you repeat the word **я** before every single verb in Ukrainian, you will sound a bit robotic and unnatural. Remember the pro-drop rule we discussed earlier. You can safely drop the pronoun if the context is clear.

Ми чита́ємо ці ре́чення. (We are reading these sentences.)
Тут ми не пи́шемо сло́во «я». (Here we do not write the word "I".)
Так ми говори́мо приро́дно. (This is how we speak naturally.)
Ми чита́ємо їх вго́лос. (We read them aloud.)

Працю́ю вдо́ма. (I work at home.)
Працю́ю бага́то. (I work a lot.)
Працю́ю над проє́ктом. (I am working on a project.)
Слу́хаю джаз. (I am listening to jazz.)
Слу́хаю ува́жно. (I listen carefully.)
Ду́маю про це. (I am thinking about it.)
Ду́маю бага́то. (I think a lot.)
Гра́ю в і́гри. (I play games.)
Гра́ю на гіта́рі. (I play the guitar.)

> [!tip]
> Пропуска́ти за́йменники — це норма́льно. (Dropping pronouns is normal.)
> Без сло́ва «я» ва́ша мо́ва звучи́ть більш приро́дно. (Without the word "я", your speech sounds more natural.)

We can also build sentences with objects. For now, we will use objects in their correct forms without worrying about all the complex case rules—just learn these common combinations as set phrases to build sentences easily and confidently.

Чита́ю текст. (I am reading a text.)
Чита́ю вго́лос. (I am reading aloud.)
Чита́ю нови́ни. (I am reading the news.)
Пишу́ листа́. (I am writing a letter.)
Пишу́ повідо́млення. (I am writing a message.)
Га́рно пишу́. (I write beautifully.)
Слу́хаю ра́діо. (I am listening to the radio.)
Пита́ю про це. (I am asking about it.)
Чека́ю на ві́дповідь. (I am waiting for an answer.)
Вивча́ю мо́ву. (I am studying the language.)
Вивча́ю слова́. (I am studying words.)

Тепе́р ви мо́жете розказа́ти про свій день. (Now you can tell about your day.)
Напри́клад, як ви працю́єте чи відпочива́єте. (For example, how you work or rest.)

Відпочива́ю вдо́ма. (I rest at home.)
Відпочива́ю влі́тку. (I rest in the summer.)
Не розумі́ю. (I do not understand.)

By combining correctly conjugated verbs with simple objects or adverbs, you create full, meaningful sentences. You are now communicating real actions.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

Сього́дні ми ви́вчили бага́то но́вого. (Today we learned a lot of new things.)
Дієсло́ва пе́ршої дієвідмі́ни — це ключ до спілкува́ння. (First conjugation verbs are the key to communication.)

The First Conjugation pattern is a master key. By learning how to conjugate **чита́ти**, you instantly know how to conjugate hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs like **писа́ти**, **працюва́ти**, and **слу́хати**.

> [!did-you-know]
> В Украї́ні є відо́ме прислі́в'я: (In Ukraine there is a famous proverb:)
> Пти́цю пізнаю́ть по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну — по мо́ві. (A bird is known by its feathers, and a person by their speech.)
> 
> This means that the way you speak reflects who you are. Every new verb you learn helps you express your true self in Ukrainian.

Тепе́р ви мо́жете: (Now you can:)
- Говори́ти про регуля́рні та пото́чні ді́ї. (Talk about regular and current actions.)
- Будува́ти ре́чення без за́йменників. (Build sentences without pronouns.)

You learned that verbs in the present tense describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. There is no need to worry about complex tense structures just yet. You also learned how to drop unnecessary pronouns to make your sentences flow naturally like a native speaker. Now, you can confidently describe your daily activities. You can say that you work, you read, you rest, and you understand. 

Test your knowledge with these quick questions. If you can answer them, you are perfectly ready for the next step.

1. Яке́ закінчення для «ми»? (What is the ending for "we"?) — Закі́нчення **-ємо**. (The ending is -ємо.)
2. Чи завжди́ тре́ба говори́ти «я»? (Is it always necessary to say "I"?) — Ні, закінчення — це підка́зка. (No, the ending is a hint.)
3. Як знайти́ осно́ву сло́ва «чита́ти»? (How to find the stem of the word "читати"?) — Ми відкида́ємо **-ти**. Осно́ва — **чита-**. (We drop -ти. The stem is "чита-".)
4. Як сказа́ти "I am reading"? (How to say "I am reading"?) — Чита́ю. (I am reading.)

Keep practicing these verbs in your daily life. Every time you perform an action, try to name it in Ukrainian. Your journey into the living language has officially begun.

Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.

      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

| You (informal) | ти | чита- + -єш | **ти чита́єш** | You read / You are reading |
| He/She/It | він/вона́/воно́ | чита- + -є | **він чита́є** | He reads / He is reading |
| We | ми | чита- + -ємо | **ми чита́ємо** | We read / We are reading |
| You (formal/plural) | ви | чита- + -єте | **ви чита́єте** | You read / You are reading |
| They | вони́ | чита- + -ють | **вони́ чита́ють** | They read / They are reading |

Це пра́вило ді́є для бага́тьох дієслі́в. (This rule works for many verbs.)
Сло́во закінчується на -ати? (Does the word end in -ати?) Ви зна́єте пра́вило! (You know the rule!)

> [!note]
> Notice that Ukrainian verbs in the present tense do two jobs at once. They describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. The phrase **я чита́ю** means both "I read every day" and "I am reading right now". This is called the imperfective aspect. You do not need a completely separate tense for ongoing actions like you do in English.

В украї́нській мо́ві є секре́т. (In the Ukrainian language there is a secret.)
Займе́нники (я, ти, ми) мо́жна пропуска́ти. (Pronouns (I, you, we) can be omitted.)
Закінчення — це підка́зка. (The ending is a hint.)
Замі́сть «я чита́ю» ми ча́сто ка́жемо просто «чита́ю». (Instead of "я читаю" we often just say "читаю".)
Це звучи́ть ду́же приро́дно. (This sounds very natural.)

Let us look at another very common verb: **зна́ти** (to know). 
Ми відкида́ємо -ти. (We drop -ти.)
Осно́ва сло́ва — «зна-». (The word stem is "зна-".)

Я зна́ю все. (I know everything.)
Вона́ зна́є текст. (She knows the text.)
Ми не зна́ємо. (We do not know.)

## Практика (Practice)

Час для пра́ктики! (Time for practice!)
Ми вчи́мося на поми́лках. (We learn from mistakes.)
Ось типо́ві поми́лки. (Here are common mistakes.)

The first common mistake is "Infinitive Abuse". Because English verbs do not change much, learners often try to use the basic dictionary form in sentences. They try to translate "I read" word-for-word and say "Я читати." This is incorrect. It sounds like "I to read." You must always conjugate the verb to match the person.

Перша пробле́ма — це інфініти́в. (The first problem is the infinitive.)
В англі́йській мо́ві слова́ не змі́нюються. (In English, words do not change.)
Лю́ди ча́сто ка́жуть «я чита́ти». (People often say "I to read".)
Це поми́лка. (This is a mistake.)
Ми за́вжди змі́нюємо дієсло́во. (We always change the verb.)

Incorrect: Я розумі́ти все. (I to understand everything.)
Correct: Я розумі́ю все. (I understand everything.)

Incorrect: Ми чека́ти на авто́бус. (We to wait for the bus.)
Correct: Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. (We are waiting for the bus.)

The second common mistake is "Pronoun Overuse". When you speak English, you constantly say "I do this, I do that." If you repeat the word **я** before every single verb in Ukrainian, you will sound a bit robotic and unnatural. Remember the pro-drop rule we discussed earlier. You can safely drop the pronoun if the context is clear.

Ми чита́ємо ці ре́чення. (We are reading these sentences.)
Тут ми не пи́шемо сло́во «я». (Here we do not write the word "I".)
Так ми говори́мо приро́дно. (This is how we speak naturally.)
Ми чита́ємо їх вго́лос. (We read them aloud.)

Працю́ю вдо́ма. (I work at home.)
Працю́ю бага́то. (I work a lot.)
Працю́ю над проє́ктом. (I am working on a project.)
Слу́хаю джаз. (I am listening to jazz.)
Слу́хаю ува́жно. (I listen carefully.)
Ду́маю про це. (I am thinking about it.)
Ду́маю бага́то. (I think a lot.)
Гра́ю в і́гри. (I play games.)
Гра́ю на гіта́рі. (I play the guitar.)

> [!tip]
> Пропуска́ти за́йменники — це норма́льно. (Dropping pronouns is normal.)
> Без сло́ва «я» ва́ша мо́ва звучи́ть більш приро́дно. (Without the word "я", your speech sounds more natural.)

We can also build sentences with objects. For now, we will use objects in their correct forms without worrying about all the complex case rules—just learn these common combinations as set phrases to build sentences easily and confidently.

Чита́ю текст. (I am reading a text.)
Чита́ю вго́лос. (I am reading aloud.)
Чита́ю нови́ни. (I am reading the news.)
Пишу́ листа́. (I am writing a letter.)
Пишу́ повідо́млення. (I am writing a message.)
Га́рно пишу́. (I write beautifully.)
Слу́хаю ра́діо. (I am listening to the radio.)
Пита́ю про це. (I am asking about it.)
Чека́ю на ві́дповідь. (I am waiting for an answer.)
Вивча́ю мо́ву. (I am studying the language.)
Вивча́ю слова́. (I am studying words.)

Тепе́р ви мо́жете розказа́ти про свій день. (Now you can tell about your day.)
Напри́клад, як ви працю́єте чи відпочива́єте. (For example, how you work or rest.)

Відпочива́ю вдо́ма. (I rest at home.)
Відпочива́ю влі́тку. (I rest in the summer.)
Не розумі́ю. (I do not understand.)

By combining correctly conjugated verbs with simple objects or adverbs, you create full, meaningful sentences. You are now communicating real actions.

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

Сього́дні ми ви́вчили бага́то но́вого. (Today we learned a lot of new things.)
Дієсло́ва пе́ршої дієвідмі́ни — це ключ до спілкува́ння. (First conjugation verbs are the key to communication.)

The First Conjugation pattern is a master key. By learning how to conjugate **чита́ти**, you instantly know how to conjugate hundreds of everyday Ukrainian verbs like **писа́ти**, **працюва́ти**, and **слу́хати**.

> [!did-you-know]
> В Украї́ні є відо́ме прислі́в'я: (In Ukraine there is a famous proverb:)
> Пти́цю пізнаю́ть по пі́р'ю, а люди́ну — по мо́ві. (A bird is known by its feathers, and a person by their speech.)
> 
> This means that the way you speak reflects who you are. Every new verb you learn helps you express your true self in Ukrainian.

Тепе́р ви мо́жете: (Now you can:)
- Говори́ти про регуля́рні та пото́чні ді́ї. (Talk about regular and current actions.)
- Будува́ти ре́чення без за́йменників. (Build sentences without pronouns.)

You learned that verbs in the present tense describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. There is no need to worry about complex tense structures just yet. You also learned how to drop unnecessary pronouns to make your sentences flow naturally like a native speaker. Now, you can confidently describe your daily activities. You can say that you work, you read, you rest, and you understand. 

Test your knowledge with these quick questions. If you can answer them, you are perfectly ready for the next step.

1. Яке́ закінчення для «ми»? (What is the ending for "we"?) — Закі́нчення **-ємо**. (The ending is -ємо.)
2. Чи завжди́ тре́ба говори́ти «я»? (Is it always necessary to say "I"?) — Ні, закінчення — це підка́зка. (No, the ending is a hint.)
3. Як знайти́ осно́ву сло́ва «чита́ти»? (How to find the stem of the word "читати"?) — Ми відкида́ємо **-ти**. Осно́ва — **чита-**. (We drop -ти. The stem is "чита-".)
4. Як сказа́ти "I am reading"? (How to say "I am reading"?) — Чита́ю. (I am reading.)

Keep practicing these verbs in your daily life. Every time you perform an action, try to name it in Ukrainian. Your journey into the living language has officially begun.

# The Living Verb I

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Verbs are the driving force of any language. Mastering the most common conjugation pattern unlocks your ability to describe your daily life, actions, and habits.

## Вступ (Introduction)

Віта́ємо на ново́му ета́пі! (Welcome to a new stage!) 
Ми вже зна́ємо слова́ про ре́чі. (We already know words about things.)
Тепе́р час для ді́ї! (Now it is time for action!)
Сього́дні ми вивча́ємо дієсло́ва. (Today we are studying verbs.)
Дієсло́ва опи́сують на́ші ді́ї. (Verbs describe our actions.)

Until now, we've focused on naming things (nouns). Now it's time for action. Verbs are the engine of Ukrainian sentences—they describe what you do, feel, and experience every day. 

Ми вивча́ємо найважливі́ші слова́: чита́ти, писа́ти і працюва́ти. (We are studying the most important words: to read, to write, and to work.)
Це на́ше щоде́нне життя́. (This is our daily life.)
Ці слова́ потрі́бні для робо́ти і хо́бі. (These words are needed for work and hobbies.)

> [!culture]
> В Украї́ні є стара́ тради́ція. (In Ukraine there is an old tradition.)
> Пе́рша відо́ма кни́га — це «Апо́стол». (The first known book is the "Apostol".)
> Іва́н Фе́доров — відо́мий друка́р. (Ivan Fedorov is a famous printer.)
>
> The first known printed book in Ukraine, the *Apostol*, was published in Lviv in 1574. Because of this deep-rooted tradition, reading is a great starting point for our verbs!

Let's use **чита́ти** (to read) to see how Ukrainian verbs work. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to talk about your daily actions.

## Презентація (Presentation)

Украї́нські дієсло́ва ма́ють нові́ закінчення. (Ukrainian verbs have new endings.)
Це зале́жить від осо́би. (This depends on the person.)
В англі́йській мо́ві це іна́кше. (In English, this is different.)
Тут ко́жна осо́ба ма́є своє́ закінчення. (Here every person has its own ending.)

Це пра́вило назива́ється «дієвідмі́на». (This rule is called "conjugation".)
Більшість украї́нських дієслі́в закінчується на -ати. (Most Ukrainian verbs end in -ати.)
Це пе́рша дієвідмі́на. (This is the first conjugation.)
Напри́клад, сло́во «чита́ти». (For example, the word "читати".)

Verbs change their endings based on who is doing the action—this is called **conjugation**. The dictionary form of a verb is called the **infinitive**. Our first group of verbs are the "-ати" verbs, because their infinitive ends in those three letters, like **чита́ти** (to read).

Де осно́ва сло́ва? (Where is the word stem?)
Ми відкида́ємо закінчення -ти. (We drop the -ти ending.)
Осно́ва сло́ва «чита́ти» — «чита-». (The stem of the word "читати" is "чита-".)
До осно́ви ми додає́мо нові́ закінчення. (We add new endings to the stem.)

To conjugate a verb, simply drop the **-ти** from the infinitive to find the stem (чита-), and then add the ending that matches the person performing the action.

Ось нові́ закінчення. (Here are the new endings.)
- Я (I) takes the ending **-ю**.
- Ти (You, informal) takes the ending **-єш**.
- Він, вона́, воно́ (He, she, it) takes the ending **-є**.
- Ми (We) takes the ending **-ємо**.
- Ви (You, formal/plural) takes the ending **-єте**.
- Вони́ (They) take the ending **-ють**.

Let us apply this rule to our main verb.

| Person | Pronoun | Stem + Ending | Result | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------------|--------|---------|
| I | я | чита- + -ю | **я чита́ю** | I read / I am reading |
| You (informal) | ти | чита- + -єш | **ти чита́єш** | You read / You are reading |
| He/She/It | він/вона́/воно́ | чита- + -є | **він чита́є** | He reads / He is reading |
| We | ми | чита- + -ємо | **ми чита́ємо** | We read / We are reading |
| You (formal/plural) | ви | чита- + -єте | **ви чита́єте** | You read / You are reading |
| They | вони́ | чита- + -ють | **вони́ чита́ють** | They read / They are reading |

Це пра́вило ді́є для бага́тьох дієслі́в. (This rule works for many verbs.)
Сло́во закінчується на -ати? (Does the word end in -ати?) Ви зна́єте пра́вило! (You know the rule!)

> [!note]
> Notice that Ukrainian verbs in the present tense do two jobs at once. They describe both habitual actions and ongoing actions. The phrase **я чита́ю** means both "I read every day" and "I am reading right now". This is called the imperfective aspect. You do not need a completely separate tense for ongoing actions like you do in English.

В украї́нській мо́ві є секре́т. (In the Ukrainian language there is a secret.)
Займе́нники (я, ти, ми) мо́жна пропуска́ти. (Pronouns (I, you, we) can be omitted.)
Закінчення — це підка́зка. (The ending is a hint.)

Це звучи́ть ду́же приро́дно. (This sounds very natural.)

Let us look at another very common verb: **зна́ти** (to know). 
Ми відкида́ємо -ти. (We drop -ти.)
Осно́ва сло́ва — «зна-». (The word stem is "зна-".)

Я зна́ю все. (I know everything.)
Вона́ зна́є текст. (She knows the text.)
Ми не зна́ємо. (We do not know.)

## Практика (Practice)

Час для пра́ктики! (Time for practice!)
Ми вчи́мося на поми́лках. (We learn from mistakes.)
Ось типо́ві поми́лки. (Here are common mistakes.)

The first common mistake is "Infinitive Abuse". Because English verbs do not change much, learners often try to use the basic dictionary form in sentences. They try to translate "I read" word-for-word and say "Я читати." This is incorrect. It sounds like "I to read." You must always conjugate the verb to match the person.

Це поми́лка. (This is a mistake.)
Ми за́вжди змі́нюємо дієсло́во. (We always change the verb.)

Incorrect: Я розумі́ти все. (I to understand everything.)
Correct: Я розумі́ю все. (I understand everything.)

Incorrect: Ми чека́ти на авто́бус. (We to wait for the bus.)
Correct: Ми чека́ємо на авто́бус. (We are waiting for the bus.)

The second common mistake is "Pronoun Overuse". When you speak English, you constantly say "I do this, I do that." If you repeat the word **я** before every single verb in Ukrainian, you will sound a bit robotic and unnatural. Remember the pro-drop rule we discussed earlier. You can safely drop the pronoun if the context is clear.

Ми чита́ємо ці ре́чення. (We are reading these sentences.)
Тут ми не пи́шемо сло́во «я». (Here we do not write the word "I".)
Так ми говори́мо приро́дно. (This is how we speak naturally.)
Ми чита́ємо їх вго́лос. (We read them aloud.)

Працю́ю вдо́ма. (I work at home.)
Працю́ю бага́то. (I work a lot.)
Працю́ю над проє́ктом. (I am working on a project.)
Слу́хаю джаз. (I am listening to jazz.)
Слу́хаю ува́жно. (I listen carefully.)
Ду́маю про це. (I am thinking about it.)
Ду́маю бага́то. (I think a lot.)
Гра́ю в і́гри. (I play games.)
Гра́ю на гіта́рі. (I play the guitar.)

> [!tip]
> Пропуска́ти за́йменники — це норма́льно. (Dropping pronouns is normal.)
> Без сло́ва «я» все звучи́ть приро́дно. (Without the word "я", everything sounds natural.)

We can also build sentences with objects. For now, we will use objects in their correct forms without worrying about all the complex case rules—just learn these common combinations as set phrases to build sentences easily and confidently.

Чита́ю текст. (I am reading a text.)
Чита́ю вго́лос. (I am reading aloud.)
Чита́ю нови́ни. (I am reading the news.)
Пишу́ листа́. (I am writing a letter.)
Пишу́ повідо́млення. (I am writing a message.)
Га́рно пишу́. (I write beautifully.)
Слу́хаю ра́діо. (I am listening to the radio.)
Пита́ю про це. (I am asking about it.)
Чека́ю на ві́дповідь. (I am waiting for an answer.)
Вивча́ю мо́ву. (I am studying the language.)
Вивча́ю слова́. (I am studying words.)

Тепе́р ви мо́жете розказа́ти про свій день. (Now you can tell about your day.)
Напри́клад, як ви працю́єте чи відпочива́єте. (For example, how you work or rest.)

Відпочива́ю вдо́ма. (I rest at home.)
Відпочива́ю влі́тку. (I rest in the summer.)

## Підсумок та культурний аспект (Summary and Cultural Insight)

Сього́дні ми ви́вчили бага́то но́вого. (Today we learned a lot of new things.)

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

- type: true-false
  title: "Grammar Rules: True or False?"
  instruction: "Read the statement about Ukrainian verbs and decide if it is true or false."
  items:
    - statement: "You must always write the word я before the verb when talking about yourself."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Because of the pro-drop rule, you can often omit the pronoun я. The ending -ю tells the listener who is performing the action."
    - statement: "The ending of the verb changes depending on who is performing the action."
      correct: true
      explanation: "This rule is called conjugation. Each person has a specific ending."
    - statement: "The phrase 'Я читаю' can mean both 'I read' and 'I am reading'."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Ukrainian present tense describes both ongoing actions and habitual actions."
    - statement: "The infinitive form читати means 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The infinitive читати means 'to read'. You must change the ending to -ю to say 'I read' (читаю)."
    - statement: "For the pronoun ми (we), the correct verb ending is -ємо."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for we is -ємо, for example, читаємо."
    - statement: "To say 'they work', you use the ending -ють."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for they is -ють, for example, працюють."
    - statement: "You need a separate tense to describe actions happening right now in Ukrainian."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The present tense is used for both habitual actions and actions happening right now."
    - statement: "'Я читати' is the correct and natural way to say 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "This is a common mistake called Infinitive Abuse. You must conjugate the verb: Я читаю."

  items:

    - words: ["Ми", "слухаємо", "радіо"]

      answer: "Вони розуміють все"

      answer: "Ви вивчаєте мову"
    - words: ["Ти", "чекаєш", "на", "автобус"]
      answer: "Ти чекаєш на автобус"
    - words: ["Вона", "пише", "листа"]
      answer: "Вона пише листа"
    - words: ["Я", "граю", "в", "ігри"]
      answer: "Я граю в ігри"

  groups:

      items: ["читаю", "знаю", "працюю", "слухаю"]

    - name: "Вони (They)"

- type: match-up

    - question: "How do you say 'She writes'?"
      options:

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

- type: true-false
  title: "Grammar Rules: True or False?"
  instruction: "Read the statement about Ukrainian verbs and decide if it is true or false."
  items:
    - statement: "You must always write the word я before the verb when talking about yourself."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Because of the pro-drop rule, you can often omit the pronoun я. The ending -ю tells the listener who is performing the action."
    - statement: "The ending of the verb changes depending on who is performing the action."
      correct: true
      explanation: "This rule is called conjugation. Each person has a specific ending."
    - statement: "The phrase 'Я читаю' can mean both 'I read' and 'I am reading'."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Ukrainian present tense describes both ongoing actions and habitual actions."
    - statement: "The infinitive form читати means 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The infinitive читати means 'to read'. You must change the ending to -ю to say 'I read' (читаю)."
    - statement: "For the pronoun ми (we), the correct verb ending is -ємо."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for we is -ємо, for example, читаємо."
    - statement: "To say 'they work', you use the ending -ють."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for they is -ють, for example, працюють."
    - statement: "You need a separate tense to describe actions happening right now in Ukrainian."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The present tense is used for both habitual actions and actions happening right now."
    - statement: "'Я читати' is the correct and natural way to say 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "This is a common mistake called Infinitive Abuse. You must conjugate the verb: Я читаю."

    - question: "How do you say 'they play'?"
      options:
        - text: "грають"
          correct: true
        - text: "граємо"
          correct: false
        - text: "грає"
          correct: false
        - text: "граю"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The ending -ють is for the pronoun вони (they)."
    - question: "How do you say 'you ask' (informal, singular)?"
      options:
        - text: "питаєш"
          correct: true
        - text: "питаю"
          correct: false
        - text: "питаєте"
          correct: false
        - text: "питає"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The ending -єш is for the pronoun ти (you, informal)."

- type: fill-in
  title: "Complete the Sentence with the Correct Verb Form"
  instruction: "Select the correct conjugated verb to complete the sentence."
  items:
    - sentence: "Я ___ книгу."
      answer: "читаю"
      options: ["читаю", "читаєш", "читає", "читаємо"]
      explanation: "The pronoun я takes the -ю ending."
    - sentence: "Він ___ музику."
      answer: "слухає"
      options: ["слухає", "слухаю", "слухаєш", "слухають"]
      explanation: "The pronoun він takes the -є ending."
    - sentence: "Вона ___ вдома."
      answer: "працює"
      options: ["працює", "працюю", "працюємо", "працюють"]
      explanation: "The pronoun вона takes the -є ending."
    - sentence: "Ми ___ слова."
      answer: "вивчаємо"
      options: ["вивчаємо", "вивчаю", "вивчає", "вивчають"]
      explanation: "The pronoun ми takes the -ємо ending."
    - sentence: "Ви ___ все."
      answer: "розумієте"
      options: ["розумієте", "розумію", "розуміє", "розуміють"]
      explanation: "The pronoun ви takes the -єте ending."
    - sentence: "Вони ___ на автобус."
      answer: "чекають"
      options: ["чекають", "чекаю", "чекає", "чекаємо"]
      explanation: "The pronoun вони takes the -ють ending."
    - sentence: "Ти ___ багато."
      answer: "думаєш"
      options: ["думаєш", "думаю", "думає", "думають"]
      explanation: "The pronoun ти takes the -єш ending."
    - sentence: "Я ___ влітку."
      answer: "відпочиваю"
      options: ["відпочиваю", "відпочиваєш", "відпочиває", "відпочивають"]
      explanation: "The pronoun я takes the -ю ending."

- type: true-false
  title: "Grammar Rules: True or False?"
  instruction: "Read the statement about Ukrainian verbs and decide if it is true or false."
  items:
    - statement: "You must always write the word я before the verb when talking about yourself."
      correct: false
      explanation: "Because of the pro-drop rule, you can often omit the pronoun я. The ending -ю tells the listener who is performing the action."
    - statement: "The ending of the verb changes depending on who is performing the action."
      correct: true
      explanation: "This rule is called conjugation. Each person has a specific ending."
    - statement: "The phrase 'Я читаю' can mean both 'I read' and 'I am reading'."
      correct: true
      explanation: "Ukrainian present tense describes both ongoing actions and habitual actions."
    - statement: "The infinitive form читати means 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The infinitive читати means 'to read'. You must change the ending to -ю to say 'I read' (читаю)."
    - statement: "For the pronoun ми (we), the correct verb ending is -ємо."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for we is -ємо, for example, читаємо."
    - statement: "To say 'they work', you use the ending -ють."
      correct: true
      explanation: "The ending for they is -ють, for example, працюють."
    - statement: "You need a separate tense to describe actions happening right now in Ukrainian."
      correct: false
      explanation: "The present tense is used for both habitual actions and actions happening right now."
    - statement: "'Я читати' is the correct and natural way to say 'I read'."
      correct: false
      explanation: "This is a common mistake called Infinitive Abuse. You must conjugate the verb: Я читаю."

- type: unjumble
  title: "Put the Words in Order"
  instruction: "Arrange the words to form a correct Ukrainian sentence."
  items:
    - words: ["Я", "читаю", "книгу"]
      answer: "Я читаю книгу"
    - words: ["Ми", "слухаємо", "радіо"]
      answer: "Ми слухаємо радіо"
    - words: ["Він", "працює", "вдома"]
      answer: "Він працює вдома"
    - words: ["Вони", "розуміють", "все"]
      answer: "Вони розуміють все"
    - words: ["Ви", "вивчаєте", "мову"]
      answer: "Ви вивчаєте мову"
    - words: ["Ти", "чекаєш", "на", "автобус"]
      answer: "Ти чекаєш на автобус"
    - words: ["Вона", "пише", "листа"]
      answer: "Вона пише листа"
    - words: ["Я", "граю", "в", "ігри"]
      answer: "Я граю в ігри"

- type: group-sort
  title: "Sort the Verb Forms"
  instruction: "Sort the verbs into the correct group based on the pronoun they belong to."
  groups:
    - name: "Я (I)"
      items: ["читаю", "знаю", "працюю", "слухаю"]
    - name: "Ми (We)"
      items: ["читаємо", "знаємо", "працюємо", "слухаємо"]
    - name: "Вони (They)"
      items: ["читають", "знають", "працюють", "слухають"]

- type: match-up
  title: "Match the Verb to the Object"
  instruction: "Match each verb with its logical ending to form a common phrase."
  pairs:
    - left: "читати"
      right: "книгу"
    - left: "писати"
      right: "листа"
    - left: "слухати"
      right: "музику"
    - left: "чекати на"
      right: "автобус"
    - left: "грати в"
      right: "ігри"
    - left: "вивчати"
      right: "мову"
    - left: "працювати"
      right: "вдома"
    - left: "знати"
      right: "правду"

- type: fill-in
  title: "Find the Missing Pronoun"
  instruction: "Look at the verb ending and select the correct pronoun for the sentence."
  items:
    - sentence: "___ читаю новини."
      answer: "Я"
      options: ["Я", "Ти", "Він", "Ми"]
      explanation: "The ending -ю goes with Я."
    - sentence: "___ знаєш все."
      answer: "Ти"
      options: ["Ти", "Я", "Вона", "Вони"]
      explanation: "The ending -єш goes with Ти."
    - sentence: "___ працює багато."
      answer: "Він"
      options: ["Він", "Я", "Ми", "Ви"]
      explanation: "The ending -є goes with Він (or Вона/Воно)."
    - sentence: "___ слухаємо радіо."
      answer: "Ми"
      options: ["Ми", "Я", "Ти", "Вони"]
      explanation: "The ending -ємо goes with Ми."
    - sentence: "___ розумієте це."
      answer: "Ви"
      options: ["Ви", "Я", "Вона", "Ми"]
      explanation: "The ending -єте goes with Ви."
    - sentence: "___ чекають на відповідь."
      answer: "Вони"
      options: ["Вони", "Я", "Ти", "Він"]
      explanation: "The ending -ють goes with Вони."
    - sentence: "___ пишу повідомлення."
      answer: "Я"
      options: ["Я", "Ти", "Ми", "Ви"]
      explanation: "The ending -у goes with Я."
    - sentence: "___ граємо на гітарі."
      answer: "Ми"
      options: ["Ми", "Я", "Ти", "Вона"]
      explanation: "The ending -ємо goes with Ми."

- type: quiz
  title: "Quick Translation Quiz"
  instruction: "Select the correct one-word translation for the English phrase."
  items:
    - question: "How do you say 'I am resting'?"
      options:
        - text: "Відпочиваю"
          correct: true
        - text: "Відпочиваєш"
          correct: false
        - text: "Відпочиває"
          correct: false
        - text: "Відпочивають"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -ю ending means 'I am resting'."
    - question: "How do you say 'We are studying'?"
      options:
        - text: "Вивчаємо"
          correct: true
        - text: "Вивчаю"
          correct: false
        - text: "Вивчає"
          correct: false
        - text: "Вивчаєте"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -ємо ending means 'we'."
    - question: "How do you say 'He asks'?"
      options:
        - text: "Питає"
          correct: true
        - text: "Питаю"
          correct: false
        - text: "Питаєш"
          correct: false
        - text: "Питають"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -є ending means 'he/she/it'."
    - question: "How do you say 'They think'?"
      options:
        - text: "Думають"
          correct: true
        - text: "Думаю"
          correct: false
        - text: "Думає"
          correct: false
        - text: "Думаємо"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -ють ending means 'they'."
    - question: "How do you say 'You understand' (informal, singular)?"
      options:
        - text: "Розумієш"
          correct: true
        - text: "Розумію"
          correct: false
        - text: "Розуміє"
          correct: false
        - text: "Розумієте"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -єш ending is for the informal 'you' (ти)."
    - question: "How do you say 'I play'?"
      options:
        - text: "Граю"
          correct: true
        - text: "Граєш"
          correct: false
        - text: "Грає"
          correct: false
        - text: "Граємо"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -ю ending is for 'I'."
    - question: "How do you say 'She writes'?"
      options:
        - text: "Пише"
          correct: true
        - text: "Пишу"
          correct: false
        - text: "Пишеш"
          correct: false
        - text: "Пишуть"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -є ending is for 'he/she/it'."
    - question: "How do you say 'We wait'?"
      options:
        - text: "Чекаємо"
          correct: true
        - text: "Чекаю"
          correct: false
        - text: "Чекає"
          correct: false
        - text: "Чекають"
          correct: false
      explanation: "The -ємо ending means 'we'."

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.
